entries.name: Memory (Core)
entries.description: File-backed memory search tools and CLI

entries.name: Telegram
entries.description: Telegram channel plugin

entries.name: canvas
entries.description: Present HTML on connected OpenClaw node canvases, navigate/eval/snapshot, and debug canvas host URLs.

entries.name: cua-driver
entries.description: Drive a native macOS app via the cua-driver CLI (default) or MCP server — snapshot its AX tree, click/type/scroll by element_index, verify via re-snapshot. Use when the user asks you to operate, drive, automate, or perform a GUI task in a real macOS application on the host (e.g. "open a file in TextEdit", "navigate to /Applications in Finder", "click the Save button in Numbers").

entries.name: diagram-maker
entries.description: Create SVG/HTML or Excalidraw diagrams for concepts, architecture, flows, and whiteboards.

entries.name: dogfood
entries.description: Systematic exploratory QA testing of web applications — find bugs, capture evidence, and generate structured reports

entries.name: gh-issues
entries.description: Fetch GitHub issues, select candidates, spawn background fix agents, open PRs, and optionally process PR review comments.

entries.name: github
entries.description: GitHub CLI for issues, PRs, CI/check logs, comments, reviews, releases, repos, and gh api queries.

entries.name: gitnexus-cli
entries.description: Use when the user needs to run GitNexus CLI commands like analyze/index a repo, check status, clean the index, generate a wiki, or list indexed repos. Examples: "Index this repo", "Reanalyze the codebase", "Generate a wiki"

entries.name: gitnexus-debugging
entries.description: Use when the user is debugging a bug, tracing an error, or asking why something fails. Examples: "Why is X failing?", "Where does this error come from?", "Trace this bug"

entries.name: gitnexus-exploring
entries.description: Use when the user asks how code works, wants to understand architecture, trace execution flows, or explore unfamiliar parts of the codebase. Examples: "How does X work?", "What calls this function?", "Show me the auth flow"

entries.name: gitnexus-guide
entries.description: Use when the user asks about GitNexus itself — available tools, how to query the knowledge graph, MCP resources, graph schema, or workflow reference. Examples: "What GitNexus tools are available?", "How do I use GitNexus?"

entries.name: gitnexus-impact-analysis
entries.description: Use when the user wants to know what will break if they change something, or needs safety analysis before editing code. Examples: "Is it safe to change X?", "What depends on this?", "What will break?"

entries.name: gitnexus-pr-review
entries.description: Use when the user wants to review a pull request, understand what a PR changes, assess risk of merging, or check for missing test coverage. Examples: "Review this PR", "What does PR #42 change?", "Is this PR safe to merge?"

entries.name: gitnexus-refactoring
entries.description: Use when the user wants to rename, extract, split, move, or restructure code safely. Examples: "Rename this function", "Extract this into a module", "Refactor this class", "Move this to a separate file"

entries.name: gortex-debug
entries.description: Use when the user is debugging a bug, tracing an error, or asking why something fails. Examples: "Why is X failing?", "Where does this error come from?", "Trace this bug"

entries.name: gortex-explore
entries.description: Use when the user asks how code works, wants to understand architecture, trace execution flows, or explore unfamiliar parts of the codebase. Examples: "How does X work?", "What calls this function?", "Show me the auth flow"

entries.name: gortex-guide
entries.description: Use when the user asks about Gortex — available tools, graph schema, or workflow reference. Examples: "What Gortex tools are available?", "How do I use Gortex?"

entries.name: gortex-impact
entries.description: Use when the user wants to know what will break if they change something, or needs safety analysis before editing code. Examples: "Is it safe to change X?", "What depends on this?", "What will break?"

entries.name: gortex-refactor
entries.description: Use when the user wants to rename, extract, split, move, or restructure code safely. Examples: "Rename this function", "Extract this into a module", "Refactor this class"

entries.name: healthcheck
entries.description: Audit/harden OpenClaw hosts: SSH, firewall, updates, exposure, backups, disk encryption, gateway security.

entries.name: kimi-desktop-gateway-policy
entries.description:
Kimi 桌面端管理 openclaw gateway 子进程生命周期。当用户提到"重启 / 启动 / 停止
openclaw / openclaw 卡住 / gateway 没响应"等需要操控 daemon 进程时，必读此 skill。
说明哪些 CLI 不能用、以及正确的恢复路径。

entries.name: kimi-webbridge
entries.description: Kimi WebBridge lets AI control the user's real browser — navigate, click, type, read, screenshot, and interact with any website using the user's actual login sessions. Use this skill whenever the user wants to interact with websites, automate browser tasks, scrape web content, or perform any action requiring a real browser. Also use when the user mentions "browser", "webpage", "open URL", "screenshot", or asks to read/interact with any website. Use even for simple-sounding browser requests — the daemon handles all complexity.

entries.name: kimiim-cli
entries.description: Use this skill for any interaction with Kimi Group Chat or its Sessions, including reading Group Rules, checking members and recent messages, replying in group/thread context, and handling Kimi IM files. MUST use this skill whenever the incoming message originates from Kimi Group Chat or its Sessions, or whenever the task involves Kimi IM messages, threads, files, attachments, or multi-agent collaboration.

entries.name: meme-maker
entries.description: Search meme templates, suggest formats, and generate local or hosted image memes.

entries.name: node-connect
entries.description: Diagnose OpenClaw Android, iOS, or macOS node pairing, QR/setup code, route, auth, and connection failures.

entries.name: node-inspect-debugger
entries.description: Debug Node.js with node inspect, --inspect, breakpoints, CDP, heap, and CPU profiles.

entries.name: python-debugpy
entries.description: Debug Python with pdb, breakpoint(), post-mortem inspection, and debugpy remote attach.

entries.name: session-logs
entries.description: Search and analyze your own session logs (older/parent conversations) using jq.

entries.name: skill-creator
entries.description: Create, edit, audit, tidy, validate, or restructure AgentSkills and SKILL.md files.

entries.name: spike
entries.description: Run throwaway prototypes to validate feasibility, compare approaches, and report a verdict.

entries.name: taskflow
entries.description: Coordinate multi-step detached tasks as one durable TaskFlow job with owner context, state, waits, and child tasks.

entries.name: taskflow-inbox-triage
entries.description: Example TaskFlow pattern for inbox triage, intent routing, waiting on replies, and later summaries.

entries.name: video-frames
entries.description: Extract frames or short clips from videos using ffmpeg.

entries.name: weather
entries.description: Current weather and forecasts with wttr.in via curl for locations, rain, temperature, travel planning.

What settings/ elements or components were either ripped out or will be broken

tools.name: cron
tools.description:
Manage Gateway cron jobs and wake events: reminders, check-back-later, delayed follow-ups, recurring work. Do not emulate scheduling with exec sleep/process polling.

Main cron => system events for heartbeat. Isolated cron => background task in `openclaw tasks`.

ACTIONS:
- status: scheduler status
- list: jobs; includeDisabled true includes disabled; agentId filter auto-filled from session
- get: one job; needs jobId
- add: create job; needs job object
- update: patch job; needs jobId + patch
- remove: delete job; needs jobId
- run: trigger now; needs jobId
- runs: run history; needs jobId
- wake: send wake event; needs text, optional mode

JOB SCHEMA (for add action):
{
  "name": "string",
  "schedule": { ... },      // required
  "payload": { ... },       // required
  "delivery": { ... },      // optional announce for isolated/current/session, webhook for any target
  "sessionTarget": "main" | "isolated" | "current" | "session:<id>",
  "enabled": true | false   // default true
}

SESSION TARGET OPTIONS:
- "main": main session; requires payload.kind="systemEvent"
- "isolated": ephemeral isolated session; requires payload.kind="agentTurn"
- "current": bind current session at creation
- "session:<id>": persistent named session

DEFAULTS:
- payload.kind="systemEvent" → defaults to "main"
- payload.kind="agentTurn" → defaults to "isolated"
Current binding needs sessionTarget="current".

SCHEDULE TYPES (schedule.kind):
- "at": one-shot absolute time
  { "kind": "at", "at": "<ISO-8601 timestamp>" }
- "every": recurring interval
  { "kind": "every", "everyMs": <ms>, "anchorMs": <optional-ms> }
- "cron": expr in supplied timezone, or Gateway host local timezone when tz omitted
  { "kind": "cron", "expr": "<cron-expression>", "tz": "<optional-IANA-timezone>" }
  Write expr in local wall-clock time; do not convert the requested local time to UTC first.
  tz omitted => Gateway host local timezone, not UTC.
  Example 6pm Shanghai daily: { "kind": "cron", "expr": "0 18 * * *", "tz": "Asia/Shanghai" }

For "at", ISO timestamps without timezone are UTC.

PAYLOAD TYPES (payload.kind):
- "systemEvent": inject text as system event
  { "kind": "systemEvent", "text": "<message>" }
- "agentTurn": run agent with prompt; isolated/current/session only
  { "kind": "agentTurn", "message": "<prompt>", "model": "<optional>", "thinking": "<optional>", "timeoutSeconds": <optional, 0=no timeout> }

DELIVERY (top-level):
  { "mode": "none|announce|webhook", "channel": "<optional>", "to": "<optional>", "threadId": "<optional>", "bestEffort": <optional-bool> }
  - isolated agentTurn default when omitted: "announce"
  - announce: send to chat channel; isolated/current/session only; optional channel/to
  - threadId: chat thread/topic id
  - webhook: POST finished-run event to delivery.to URL
  - Specific chat/recipient: set announce delivery.channel/to; do not call messaging tools inside run.

CRITICAL CONSTRAINTS:
- sessionTarget="main" REQUIRES payload.kind="systemEvent"
- sessionTarget="isolated" | "current" | "session:xxx" REQUIRES payload.kind="agentTurn"
- Webhook: delivery.mode="webhook" and delivery.to URL.
Default: prefer isolated agentTurn jobs unless the user explicitly wants current-session binding.

RESTRICTED CRON RUNS:
- Some isolated cron runs get narrow self-cleanup grant: status/list self-only, get/runs current job only, mutation only remove current job.

WAKE MODES (for wake action):
- "next-heartbeat" default: wake next heartbeat
- "now": wake immediately

Use jobId canonical; id accepted compat. contextMessages (0-10) adds previous messages as job context.

tools.name: edit
tools.description: Edit a single file using exact text replacement. Every edits[].oldText must match a unique, non-overlapping region of the original file. If two changes affect the same block or nearby lines, merge them into one edit instead of emitting overlapping edits. Do not include large unchanged regions just to connect distant changes.

path.description: Path to the file to edit (relative or absolute)

edits.description: One or more targeted replacements. Each edit is matched against the original file, not incrementally. Do not include overlapping or nested edits. If two changes touch the same block or nearby lines, merge them into one edit instead.

tools.name: exec
tools.description: Execute shell commands with background continuation for work that starts now. Use yieldMs/background to continue later via process tool. For long-running work started now, rely on automatic completion wake when it is enabled and the command emits output or fails; otherwise use process to confirm completion. Use process whenever you need logs, status, input, or intervention. Do not use exec sleep or delay loops for reminders or deferred follow-ups; use cron instead. Use pty=true for TTY-required commands (terminal UIs, coding agents).

timeout.description: Timeout in seconds (optional, kills process on expiry)

pty.description: Run in a pseudo-terminal (PTY) when available (TTY-required CLIs, coding agents)

elevated.description: Run on the host with elevated permissions (if allowed)

security.description: Ignored for normal calls; exec security is set by tools.exec.security and host approvals.

tools.name: image
tools.description: Analyze images with vision model. Use image for one path/URL, images for max 20. Only use this tool when images were NOT already provided; prompt images already visible.

tools.name: image_generate
tools.description: Create/edit images. Session chats: background task; do not call image_generate again for same request; wait completion, then send attachments via message tool. Transparent: outputFormat="png" or "webp" + background="transparent"; OpenAI also supports openai.background and routes default model to gpt-image-1.5. Use action="list" for providers/models/readiness/auth, "status" for active task.

model.description: Provider/model override, e.g. openai/gpt-image-2; transparent OpenAI: openai/gpt-image-1.5.

filename.description: Output filename hint; basename preserved in managed media dir.

background.description: Background: transparent, opaque, auto. Transparent needs png/webp output.

tools.name: memory_get
tools.description: Safe exact excerpt read from MEMORY.md or memory/*.md. Defaults to a bounded excerpt when lines are omitted, includes truncation/continuation info when more content exists, and `corpus=wiki` reads from registered compiled-wiki supplements.

tools.name: memory_search
tools.description: Mandatory recall step: semantically search MEMORY.md + memory/*.md (and optional session transcripts) before answering questions about prior work, decisions, dates, people, preferences, or todos. Optional `corpus=wiki` or `corpus=all` also searches registered compiled-wiki supplements. `corpus=memory` restricts hits to indexed memory files (excludes session transcript chunks from ranking). `corpus=sessions` restricts hits to indexed session transcripts (same visibility rules as session history tools). If response has disabled=true, memory retrieval is unavailable and should be surfaced to the user.

tools.name: music_generate
tools.description: Create audio/music for song, jingle, beat, loop, soundtrack, anthem, instrumental requests. If user asks make/generate/create song/music, call music_generate; do not just write lyrics unless lyrics/text only. Prompt gets style/genre/mood/tempo/instruments/purpose. lyrics only exact sung words. Session chats: background task; do not call again for same request; wait completion, send attachments via message tool. "status" checks active task.

prompt.description: Music prompt: style, genre, mood, purpose.

lyrics.description: Exact sung lyrics only when the user supplies lyrics or asks for vocal words. For song/style requests, use prompt instead.

filename.description: Output filename hint; basename preserved in managed media dir.

tools.name: process
tools.description: Manage running exec sessions for commands already started: list, poll, log, write, send-keys, submit, paste, kill. Use poll/log when you need status, logs, quiet-success confirmation, or completion confirmation when automatic completion wake is unavailable. Use poll/log also for input-wait hints. Use write/send-keys/submit/paste/kill for input or intervention. Do not use process polling to emulate timers or reminders; use cron for scheduled follow-ups.

sessionId.description: Session id for actions other than list

keys.description: Key tokens to send for send-keys

hex.description: Hex bytes to send for send-keys

timeout.description: For poll: wait up to this many milliseconds before returning; max 30000 ms, higher values are clamped to 30000

tools.name: read
tools.description: Read the contents of a file. Supports text files and images (jpg, png, gif, webp). Images are sent as attachments. For text files, output is truncated to 2000 lines or 50KB (whichever is hit first). Use offset/limit for large files. When you need the full file, continue with offset until complete.

path.description: Path to the file to read (relative or absolute)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read

tools.name: session_status
tools.description: Show /status-like card for current/visible session: model, usage, time, cost, tasks. Use `sessionKey="current"` for current session; UI labels like `openclaw-tui` are not keys. `model` sets session override; `model=default` resets. Use for active model/session config questions.

tools.name: sessions_history
tools.description: Fetch sanitized history for visible session. Use before replying, debugging, resuming; supports limits/tool messages.

tools.name: sessions_list
tools.description: List visible sessions; filter by kind, label, agentId, search, activity. Use before sessions_history or sessions_send target selection.

tools.name: sessions_send
tools.description: Send message to visible session by sessionKey/label, or configured agent by agentId. Thread-scoped chats rejected; target parent channel session. Creates missing configured-agent main session; waits for reply when available.

tools.name: sessions_spawn
tools.description: Spawn clean child session; default `runtime="subagent"`. `mode="run"` one-shot background work. Subagents inherit parent workspace. Native subagents get task in first visible `[Subagent Task]` message. Native only: `context="fork"` only when child needs current transcript; else omit or `isolated`. Use for fresh child-session work.

taskName.description: Stable alias for later targeting; lowercase letters/digits/underscores, starts letter.

context.description: Native context. Omit/"isolated" for clean child; "fork" only when child needs requester transcript.

tools.name: sessions_yield
tools.description: End current turn. Use after spawning subagents; results arrive as next message.

tools.name: subagents
tools.description: List/kill/steer spawned subagents for requester session. If sessions_yield exists, use it for completion; do not poll wait loops.

tools.name: update_plan
tools.description: Update current run plan. Use for non-trivial multi-step work; keep plan current while executing. Short steps; max one `in_progress`; skip for simple one-step work.

tools.name: video_generate
tools.description: Create videos. Session chats: background task; do not call video_generate again for same request; wait completion, then send attachments via message tool. "status" checks active task. Duration may round to provider-supported value.

imageRoles.description: `image` + `images` roles by index after de-dupe. Values: first_frame, last_frame, reference_image; empty string leaves unset.

videoRoles.description: `video` + `videos` roles by index after de-dupe. Value: reference_video; empty string leaves unset.

filename.description: Output filename hint; basename preserved in managed media dir.

aspectRatio.description: Aspect ratio: 1:1, 16:9, 9:16, "adaptive", or provider value; unsupported normalized/ignored.

resolution.description: Resolution: 480P, 720P, 768P, 1080P, 4K, or provider value; unsupported normalized/ignored.

durationSeconds.description: Target seconds; may round to nearest supported duration.

providerOptions.description: Provider JSON options, e.g. {"seed":42}. Keys/types must match provider capabilities; mismatch skips candidate. Use action=list for accepted keys.

tools.name: web_fetch
tools.description: Fetch URL and extract readable markdown/text. Lightweight page access; no browser automation.

tools.name: web_search
tools.description: Search web for current info; returns normalized provider results.

tools.name: write
tools.description: Write content to a file. Creates the file if it doesn't exist, overwrites if it does. Automatically creates parent directories.

path.description: Path to the file to write (relative or absolute)

content.description: Content to write to the file

assistantTexts: Let me check the current state of both the old Control UI and the new cockpit to understand what was migrated and what might be missing.

Here's the breakdown comparing the **old Control UI** (built-in Gateway dashboard) with the **new Cockpit** (standalone chat client):

---

## 🚨 What's Completely Gone

| Feature | Old Control UI | New Cockpit |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| **Gateway WebSocket** | Direct WS to Gateway — full chat semantics (`chat.history`, `chat.send`, `chat.abort`, `chat.inject`) | Generic HTTP `/v1/chat/completions` client only |
| **Server Management** | Config editor, live logs tail, debug panel, status/health snapshots | ❌ None |
| **Channels** | QR login, per-channel config, probe refreshes | ❌ None |
| **Instances/Presence** | Live presence list | ❌ None |
| **Sessions** | Session picker, per-session model/thinking/verbose overrides | ❌ None |
| **Cron Jobs** | Full CRUD + run history | ❌ None |
| **Skills** | Install, enable/disable, API key updates | ❌ None |
| **Nodes** | List + capabilities | ❌ None |
| **Exec Approvals** | Edit gateway/node allowlists | ❌ None |
| **Dreams/Diary** | Dreaming status, diary reader | ❌ None |
| **Update** | Package/git update + restart polling | ❌ None |
| **Voice/Talk** | Browser WebRTC, microphone, VAD, relay transport | ❌ Mic button is a **non-functional stub** |
| **Tool Streaming** | Live tool output cards | ❌ None |
| **Slash Commands** | `/new`, `/reset`, `/stop` interception | ❌ None |
| **Themes** | Claw/Knot/Dash + tweakcn import, light/dark/system | **Dark only** — no light mode, no theme switching |
| **i18n** | 20 locales, lazy-loaded | ❌ English only |
| **PWA** | Manifest, service worker, offline support | ❌ None |

---

## ⚠️ What Works Differently / Partially Broken

| Feature | Old | New |
|---------|-----|-----|
| **Chat History** | Bounded recent window, strips directives, merges optimistic tail with canonical transcript, handles oversized messages | Simple localStorage thread array — no server sync |
| **Images** | Persisted as managed media references, served through authenticated Gateway URLs | Base64 data URLs in localStorage — large, not shared, will bloat storage |
| **Auth** | Gateway token, password, Tailscale identity, trusted-proxy | Raw API key string in localStorage only |
| **Model Picker** | Dynamic provider catalogs, `agents.defaults.models` allowlist | Hardcoded text field in settings |
| **Session Identity** | Browser-local display name + avatar upload | Display name only, no avatar upload |
| **Videos** | N/A | **Empty stub page** — no actual video generation integration |

---

## ✅ What the Cockpit Actually Adds

- **Generic /v1 endpoint client** — works with any OpenAI-compatible API
- **Endpoint labeling system** — custom paths, templates, response path extraction, caching
- **Local thread management** — create, save, delete threads (localStorage only)
- **Hit/miss stats** — per-endpoint cache statistics
- **Temporary chats** — ephemeral threads that don't save
- **Offline queue** — basic message queuing when disconnected
- **Cool dark UI** — oklch-based hue-cycling accent halo

---

## 🔧 Bottom Line

The Cockpit is **not a functional replacement** for the Control UI. It's a **completely different app**:

- **Control UI** = Gateway management dashboard + chat
- **Cockpit** = Standalone chat client for generic `/v1` APIs

If you deploy this as the replacement, you lose **all server-side management capabilities** — no config editing, no logs, no cron, no skills, no channels, no voice, no session overrides. It's just a chat window.

Want me to start bridging any of these gaps, or was the intent to have a lightweight chat-only interface alongside the Control UI rather than replacing it?

entries.description:
Kimi 桌面端管理 openclaw gateway 子进程生命周期。当用户提到"重启 / 启动 / 停止
openclaw / openclaw 卡住 / gateway 没响应"等需要操控 daemon 进程时，必读此 skill。
说明哪些 CLI 不能用、以及正确的恢复路径。

entries.name: kimi-webbridge
entries.description: Kimi WebBridge lets AI control the user's real browser — navigate, click, type, read, screenshot, and interact with any website using the user's actual login sessions. Use this skill whenever the user wants to interact with websites, automate browser tasks, scrape web content, or perform any action requiring a real browser. Also use when the user mentions "browser", "webpage", "open URL", "screenshot", or asks to read/interact with any website. Use even for simple-sounding browser requests — the daemon handles all complexity.

entries.name: kimiim-cli
entries.description: Use this skill for any interaction with Kimi Group Chat or its Sessions, including reading Group Rules, checking members and recent messages, replying in group/thread context, and handling Kimi IM files. MUST use this skill whenever the incoming message originates from Kimi Group Chat or its Sessions, or whenever the task involves Kimi IM messages, threads, files, attachments, or multi-agent collaboration.

entries.name: session-logs

---
> ## Documentation Index
> Fetch the complete documentation index at: https://docs.openclaw.ai/llms.txt
> Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further.

# Control UI

The Control UI is a small **Vite + Lit** single-page app served by the Gateway:

* default: `http://<host>:18789/`
* optional prefix: set `gateway.controlUi.basePath` (e.g. `/openclaw`)

It speaks **directly to the Gateway WebSocket** on the same port.

## Quick open (local)

If the Gateway is running on the same computer, open:

* [http://127.0.0.1:18789/](http://127.0.0.1:18789/) (or [http://localhost:18789/](http://localhost:18789/))

If the page fails to load, start the Gateway first: `openclaw gateway`.

Auth is supplied during the WebSocket handshake via:

* `connect.params.auth.token`
* `connect.params.auth.password`
* Tailscale Serve identity headers when `gateway.auth.allowTailscale: true`
* trusted-proxy identity headers when `gateway.auth.mode: "trusted-proxy"`

The dashboard settings panel keeps a token for the current browser tab session and selected gateway URL; passwords are not persisted. Onboarding usually generates a gateway token for shared-secret auth on first connect, but password auth works too when `gateway.auth.mode` is `"password"`.

## Device pairing (first connection)

When you connect to the Control UI from a new browser or device, the Gateway usually requires a **one-time pairing approval**. This is a security measure to prevent unauthorized access.

**What you'll see:** "disconnected (1008): pairing required"

<Steps>
  <Step title="List pending requests">
    ```bash theme={"theme":{"light":"min-light","dark":"min-dark"}}
    openclaw devices list
    ```
  </Step>

  <Step title="Approve by request ID">
    ```bash theme={"theme":{"light":"min-light","dark":"min-dark"}}
    openclaw devices approve <requestId>
    ```
  </Step>
</Steps>

If the browser retries pairing with changed auth details (role/scopes/public key), the previous pending request is superseded and a new `requestId` is created. Re-run `openclaw devices list` before approval.

If the browser is already paired and you change it from read access to write/admin access, this is treated as an approval upgrade, not a silent reconnect. OpenClaw keeps the old approval active, blocks the broader reconnect, and asks you to approve the new scope set explicitly.

Once approved, the device is remembered and won't require re-approval unless you revoke it with `openclaw devices revoke --device <id> --role <role>`. See [Devices CLI](/cli/devices) for token rotation and revocation.

<Note>
  * Direct local loopback browser connections (`127.0.0.1` / `localhost`) are auto-approved.
  * Tailscale Serve can skip the pairing round trip for Control UI operator sessions when `gateway.auth.allowTailscale: true`, Tailscale identity verifies, and the browser presents its device identity.
  * Direct Tailnet binds, LAN browser connects, and browser profiles without device identity still require explicit approval.
  * Each browser profile generates a unique device ID, so switching browsers or clearing browser data will require re-pairing.
</Note>

## Personal identity (browser-local)

The Control UI supports a per-browser personal identity (display name and avatar) attached to outgoing messages for attribution in shared sessions. It lives in browser storage, is scoped to the current browser profile, and is not synced to other devices or persisted server-side beyond the normal transcript authorship metadata on messages you actually send. Clearing site data or switching browsers resets it to empty.

The same browser-local pattern applies to the assistant avatar override. Uploaded assistant avatars overlay the gateway-resolved identity on the local browser only and never round-trip through `config.patch`. The shared `ui.assistant.avatar` config field is still available for non-UI clients writing the field directly (such as scripted gateways or custom dashboards).

## Runtime config endpoint

The Control UI fetches its runtime settings from `/__openclaw/control-ui-config.json`. That endpoint is gated by the same gateway auth as the rest of the HTTP surface: unauthenticated browsers cannot fetch it, and a successful fetch requires either an already valid gateway token/password, Tailscale Serve identity, or a trusted-proxy identity.

## Language support

The Control UI can localize itself on first load based on your browser locale. To override it later, open **Overview -> Gateway Access -> Language**. The locale picker lives in the Gateway Access card, not under Appearance.

* Supported locales: `en`, `zh-CN`, `zh-TW`, `pt-BR`, `de`, `es`, `ja-JP`, `ko`, `fr`, `ar`, `it`, `tr`, `uk`, `id`, `pl`, `th`, `vi`, `nl`, `fa`
* Non-English translations are lazy-loaded in the browser.
* The selected locale is saved in browser storage and reused on future visits.
* Missing translation keys fall back to English.

Docs translations are generated for the same non-English locale set, but the docs site's built-in Mintlify language picker is limited to the locale codes Mintlify accepts. Thai (`th`) and Persian (`fa`) docs are still generated in the publish repo; they may not appear in that picker until Mintlify supports those codes.

## Appearance themes

The Appearance panel keeps the built-in Claw, Knot, and Dash themes, plus one browser-local tweakcn import slot. To import a theme, open [tweakcn editor](https://tweakcn.com/editor/theme), choose or create a theme, click **Share**, and paste the copied theme link into Appearance. The importer also accepts `https://tweakcn.com/r/themes/<id>` registry URLs, editor URLs like `https://tweakcn.com/editor/theme?theme=amethyst-haze`, relative `/themes/<id>` paths, raw theme IDs, and default theme names such as `amethyst-haze`.

Appearance also includes a browser-local Text size setting. The setting is stored with the rest of Control UI preferences, applies to chat text, composer text, tool cards, and chat sidebars, and keeps text inputs at least 16px so mobile Safari does not auto-zoom on focus.

Imported themes are stored only in the current browser profile. They are not written to gateway config and do not sync across devices. Replacing the imported theme updates the one local slot; clearing it switches the active theme back to Claw if the imported theme was selected.

## What it can do (today)

<AccordionGroup>
  <Accordion title="Chat and Talk">
    * Chat with the model via Gateway WS (`chat.history`, `chat.send`, `chat.abort`, `chat.inject`).
    * Chat history refreshes request a bounded recent window with per-message text caps so large sessions do not force the browser to render a full transcript payload before the chat becomes usable.
    * Talk through browser realtime sessions. OpenAI uses direct WebRTC, Google Live uses a constrained one-use browser token over WebSocket, and backend-only realtime voice plugins use the Gateway relay transport. Client-owned provider sessions start with `talk.client.create`; Gateway relay sessions start with `talk.session.create`. The relay keeps provider credentials on the Gateway while the browser streams microphone PCM through `talk.session.appendAudio` and forwards `openclaw_agent_consult` provider tool calls through `talk.client.toolCall` for Gateway policy and the larger configured OpenClaw model.
    * Stream tool calls + live tool output cards in Chat (agent events).
  </Accordion>

  <Accordion title="Channels, instances, sessions, dreams">
    * Channels: built-in plus bundled/external plugin channels status, QR login, and per-channel config (`channels.status`, `web.login.*`, `config.patch`).
    * Channel probe refreshes keep the previous snapshot visible while slow provider checks finish, and partial snapshots are labeled when a probe or audit exceeds its UI budget.
    * Instances: presence list + refresh (`system-presence`).
    * Sessions: list configured-agent sessions by default, fall back from stale unconfigured agent session keys, and apply per-session model/thinking/fast/verbose/trace/reasoning overrides (`sessions.list`, `sessions.patch`).
    * Dreams: dreaming status, enable/disable toggle, and Dream Diary reader (`doctor.memory.status`, `doctor.memory.dreamDiary`, `config.patch`).
  </Accordion>

  <Accordion title="Cron, skills, nodes, exec approvals">
    * Cron jobs: list/add/edit/run/enable/disable + run history (`cron.*`).
    * Skills: status, enable/disable, install, API key updates (`skills.*`).
    * Nodes: list + caps (`node.list`).
    * Exec approvals: edit gateway or node allowlists + ask policy for `exec host=gateway/node` (`exec.approvals.*`).
  </Accordion>

  <Accordion title="Config">
    * View/edit `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` (`config.get`, `config.set`).
    * Apply + restart with validation (`config.apply`) and wake the last active session.
    * Writes include a base-hash guard to prevent clobbering concurrent edits.
    * Writes (`config.set`/`config.apply`/`config.patch`) preflight active SecretRef resolution for refs in the submitted config payload; unresolved active submitted refs are rejected before write.
    * Form saves discard stale redacted placeholders that cannot be restored from the saved config while preserving redacted values that still map to saved secrets.
    * Schema + form rendering (`config.schema` / `config.schema.lookup`, including field `title` / `description`, matched UI hints, immediate child summaries, docs metadata on nested object/wildcard/array/composition nodes, plus plugin + channel schemas when available); Raw JSON editor is available only when the snapshot has a safe raw round-trip.
    * If a snapshot cannot safely round-trip raw text, Control UI forces Form mode and disables Raw mode for that snapshot.
    * Raw JSON editor "Reset to saved" preserves the raw-authored shape (formatting, comments, `$include` layout) instead of re-rendering a flattened snapshot, so external edits survive a reset when the snapshot can safely round-trip.
    * Structured SecretRef object values are rendered read-only in form text inputs to prevent accidental object-to-string corruption.
  </Accordion>

  <Accordion title="Debug, logs, update">
    * Debug: status/health/models snapshots + event log + manual RPC calls (`status`, `health`, `models.list`).
    * The event log includes Control UI refresh/RPC timings, slow chat/config render timings, and browser responsiveness entries for long animation frames or long tasks when the browser e

If `agents.defaults.models` is present, that allowlist drives the picker, including `provider/*` entries that keep provider-scoped catalogs dynamic. Otherwise the picker shows explicit `models.providers.*.models` entries plus providers with usable auth. The full catalog stays available through the debug `models.list` RPC with `view: "all"`.

  <Accordion title="Talk mode (browser realtime)">
    Talk mode uses a registered realtime voice provider. Configure OpenAI with `talk.realtime.provider: "openai"` plus either `talk.realtime.providers.openai.apiKey`, `OPENAI_API_KEY`, or an `openai-codex` OAuth profile; configure Google with `talk.realtime.provider: "google"` plus `talk.realtime.providers.google.apiKey`. Google Live receives a one-use constrained Live API auth token for a browser WebSocket session, with instructions and tool declarations locked into the token by the Gateway. The command prints provider status only and does not log secrets.

    * `chat.abort` supports `{ sessionKey }` (no `runId`) to abort all active runs for that session.

content.thinking:
Now I have a comprehensive understanding of both the old Control UI and the new Cockpit. Let me compile a detailed answer about what was ripped out or will be broken.

Key differences:

**Old Control UI had:**
1. **Full Gateway Integration** - Talks directly to Gateway WebSocket, not just a generic /v1 API endpoint
2. **Device Pairing** - One-time pairing approval flow
3. **Multi-language Support** - 20 locales with lazy loading
4. **Multiple Themes** - Claw, Knot, Dash + tweakcn import
5. **Light/Dark/System mode switching**
6. **Chat with full Gateway semantics** - chat.history, chat.send, chat.abort, chat.inject, idempotency, bounded history, inline directive stripping, optimistic messages, session compaction
7. **Talk/Voice mode** - Browser realtime (WebRTC, WebSocket relay), microphone, VAD options
8. **Tool call streaming** - Live tool output cards
9. **Channels management** - Built-in + plugin channels, QR login, config
10. **Channel probe refreshes**
11. **Instances/Presence list**
12. **Sessions management** - List, model/thinking/fast/verbose/trace/reasoning overrides, sessions.patch
13. **Dreams/Dream Diary**
14. **Cron jobs** - Full CRUD + run history
15. **Skills** - Status, enable/disable, install, API key updates
16. **Nodes** - List + caps
17. **Exec approvals** - Edit gateway/node allowlists
18. **Config editor** - Full config.get/set/apply with validation, schema rendering, SecretRef resolution, raw JSON editor, base-hash guards
19. **Debug panel** - Status/health/models snapshots, event log, manual RPC calls, performance observer
20. **Logs** - Live tail of gateway file logs with filter/export
21. **Update** - Package/git update + restart
22. **Personal identity/avatar** - Browser-local display name and avatar
23. **Gateway Access settings** - Language, auth token management
24. **Appearance settings** - Text size, theme import
25. **Slash commands** - /new, /reset, /stop, etc.
26. **Session picker scoped by agent**
27. **Model picker** - Dynamic provider catalogs, agents.defaults.models allowlist
28. **Context usage indicator** - Token pressure warnings, compaction button
29. **Consecutive duplicate message collapsing**
30. **PWA features** - Manifest, service worker, favicons, apple touch icon
31. **Mount fallback** - Error UI when app doesn't load
32. **Web component** - `<openclaw-app>` custom element

**New Cockpit has:**
1. **Generic /v1 API client** - Works with any OpenAI-compatible endpoint
2. **Local-only storage** - localStorage for threads/settings, no Gateway connection
3. **Endpoint labeling system** - Custom endpoints with templates, caching, response path extraction
4. **Basic chat** - Send/receive with streaming support, image attachments
5. **Thread management** - Create, select, delete threads
6. **Library view** - List saved threads
7. **Images view** - Gallery of image attachments
8. **Videos view** - Stub/placeholder
9. **Settings page** - Connection config + endpoint editor
10. **Dark-only design** - oklch-based, no light mode
11. **Animated accent halo** - Hue-cycling based on UI state
12. **Cache layer** - Per-endpoint TTL caching in localStorage
13. **Hit/miss stats** - Basic <redacted> statistics
14. **Temporary chats** - Ephemeral threads
15. **Cooldown handling** - Retry-after support
16. **Offline queue** - Basic offline message queuing

**What's ripped out / broken:**
1. No Gateway WebSocket connection - completely standalone
2. No device pairing
3. No multi-language support
4. No theme switching (only dark)
5. No voice/talk/realtime mode (Mic button is a stub)
6. No tool call streaming/display
7. No channels management
8. No instances/presence
9. No sessions management (no model override per session, no session picker)
10. No dreams
11. No cron jobs
12. No skills management
13. No nodes list
14. No exec approvals
15. No config editor
16. No debug/status panel
17. No live log tailing
18. No update mechanism
19. No slash commands
20. No agent filtering
21. No context token usage indicator
22. No consecutive duplicate collapsing
23. No PWA features (no manifest/sw)
24. No browser-local avatar upload
25. No history compaction
26. No chat.abort with sessionKey semantics
27. No chat.inject
28. No optimistic history merging with canonical transcript
29. No assistant image persistence as managed media
30. No idempotency key handling

**What will be broken if replacing Control UI:**
- All Gateway management features are gone
- It's now just a chat client, not a Gateway dashboard
- No real-time collaboration or presence
- No server-side features at all
- Voice/Mic button is non-functional stub
- Videos page is empty stub
- No server config editing
- No theme/light mode for users who prefer it

The Cockpit is essentially a standalone chat UI that happens to be hosted at the same port, not a replacement for the Gateway management dashboard that Control UI was.

I should present this clearly and concisely.

content.text:
Here's the breakdown comparing the **old Control UI** (built-in Gateway dashboard) with the **new Cockpit** (standalone chat client):

---

## 🚨 What's Completely Gone

| Feature | Old Control UI | New Cockpit |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| **Gateway WebSocket** | Direct WS to Gateway — full chat semantics (`chat.history`, `chat.send`, `chat.abort`, `chat.inject`) | Generic HTTP `/v1/chat/completions` client only |
| **Server Management** | Config editor, live logs tail, debug panel, status/health snapshots | ❌ None |
| **Channels** | QR login, per-channel config, probe refreshes | ❌ None |
| **Instances/Presence** | Live presence list | ❌ None |
| **Sessions** | Session picker, per-session model/thinking/verbose overrides | ❌ None |
| **Cron Jobs** | Full CRUD + run history | ❌ None |
| **Skills** | Install, enable/disable, API key updates | ❌ None |
| **Nodes** | List + capabilities | ❌ None |
| **Exec Approvals** | Edit gateway/node allowlists | ❌ None |
| **Dreams/Diary** | Dreaming status, diary reader | ❌ None |
| **Update** | Package/git update + restart polling | ❌ None |
| **Voice/Talk** | Browser WebRTC, microphone, VAD, relay transport | ❌ Mic button is a **non-functional stub** |
| **Tool Streaming** | Live tool output cards | ❌ None |
| **Slash Commands** | `/new`, `/reset`, `/stop` interception | ❌ None |
| **Themes** | Claw/Knot/Dash + tweakcn import, light/dark/system | **Dark only** — no light mode, no theme switching |
| **i18n** | 20 locales, lazy-loaded | ❌ English only |
| **PWA** | Manifest, service worker, offline support | ❌ None |

---

## ⚠️ What Works Differently / Partially Broken

| Feature | Old | New |
|---------|-----|-----|
| **Chat History** | Bounded recent window, strips directives, merges optimistic tail with canonical transcript, handles oversized messages | Simple localStorage thread array — no server sync |
| **Images** | Persisted as managed media references, served through authenticated Gateway URLs | Base64 data URLs in localStorage — large, not shared, will bloat storage |
| **Auth** | Gateway token, password, Tailscale identity, trusted-proxy | Raw API key string in localStorage only |
| **Model Picker** | Dynamic provider catalogs, `agents.defaults.models` allowlist | Hardcoded text field in settings |
| **Session Identity** | Browser-local display name + avatar upload | Display name only, no avatar upload |
| **Videos** | N/A | **Empty stub page** — no actual video generation integration |

---

## ✅ What the Cockpit Actually Adds

- **Generic /v1 endpoint client** — works with any OpenAI-compatible API
- **Endpoint labeling system** — custom paths, templates, response path extraction, caching
- **Local thread management** — create, save, delete threads (localStorage only)
- **Hit/miss stats** — per-endpoint cache statistics
- **Temporary chats** — ephemeral threads that don't save
- **Offline queue** — basic message queuing when disconnected
- **Cool dark UI** — oklch-based hue-cycling accent halo

---

## 🔧 Bottom Line

The Cockpit is **not a functional replacement** for the Control UI. It's a **completely different app**:

- **Control UI** = Gateway management dashboard + chat
- **Cockpit** = Standalone chat client for generic `/v1` APIs

If you deploy this as the replacement, you lose **all server-side management capabilities** — no config editing, no logs, no cron, no skills, no channels, no voice, no session overrides. It's just a chat window.

Want me to start bridging any of these gaps, or was the intent to have a lightweight chat-only interface alongside the Control UI rather than replacing it?

Use when the user asks you to operate, drive, automate, or perform a GUI task in a real macOS application on the host (e.g. "open a file in TextEdit", "navigate to /Applications in Finder", "click the Save button in Numbers").

entries.description: Use when the user is debugging a bug, tracing an error, or asking why something fails. Examples: "Why is X failing?", "Where does this error come from?", "Trace this bug"

entries.description: Use when the user asks how code works, wants to understand architecture, trace execution flows, or explore unfamiliar parts of the codebase. Examples: "How does X work?", "What calls this function?", "Show me the auth flow"

entries.description: Use when the user asks about GitNexus itself — available tools, how to query the knowledge graph, MCP resources, graph schema, or workflow reference. Examples: "What GitNexus tools are available?", "How do I use GitNexus?"

entries.description: Use when the user wants to know what will break if they change something, or needs safety analysis before editing code. Examples: "Is it safe to change X?", "What depends on this?", "What will break?"

entries.description: Use when the user wants to review a pull request, understand what a PR changes, assess risk of merging, or check for missing test coverage. Examples: "Review this PR", "What does PR #42 change?", "Is this PR safe to merge?"

Kimi 桌面端管理 openclaw gateway 子进程生命周期。当用户提到"重启 / 启动 / 停止
openclaw / openclaw 卡住 / gateway 没响应"等需要操控 daemon 进程时，必读此 skill。
说明哪些 CLI 不能用、以及正确的恢复路径。

entries.name: kimi-webbridge
entries.description: Kimi WebBridge lets AI control the user's real browser — navigate, click, type, read, screenshot, and interact with any website using the user's actual login sessions. Use this skill whenever the user wants to interact with websites, automate browser tasks, scrape web content, or perform any action requiring a real browser. Also use when the user mentions "browser", "webpage", "open URL", "screenshot", or asks to read/interact with any website. Use even for simple-sounding browser requests — the daemon handles all complexity.

entries.name: kimiim-cli
entries.description: Use this skill for any interaction with Kimi Group Chat or its Sessions, including reading Group Rules, checking members and recent messages, replying in group/thread context, and handling Kimi IM files. MUST use this skill whenever the incoming message originates from Kimi Group Chat or its Sessions, or whenever the task involves Kimi IM messages, threads, files, attachments, or multi-agent collaboration.

---
> ## Documentation Index
> Fetch the complete documentation index at: https://docs.openclaw.ai/llms.txt
> Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further.

# Control UI

The Control UI is a small **Vite + Lit** single-page app served by the Gateway:

* default: `http://<host>:18789/`
* optional prefix: set `gateway.controlUi.basePath` (e.g. `/openclaw`)

It speaks **directly to the Gateway WebSocket** on the same port.

## Quick open (local)

If the Gateway is running on the same computer, open:

* [http://127.0.0.1:18789/](http://127.0.0.1:18789/) (or [http://localhost:18789/](http://localhost:18789/))

If the page fails to load, start the Gateway first: `openclaw gateway`.

Auth is supplied during the WebSocket handshake via:

* `connect.params.auth.token`
* `connect.params.auth.password`
* Tailscale Serve identity headers when `gateway.auth.allowTailscale: true`
* trusted-proxy identity headers when `gateway.auth.mode: "trusted-proxy"`

The dashboard settings panel keeps a token for the current browser tab session and selected gateway URL; passwords are not persisted. Onboarding usually generates a gateway token for shared-secret auth on first connect, but password auth works too when `gateway.auth.mode` is `"password"`.

## Device pairing (first connection)

When you connect to the Control UI from a new browser or device, the Gateway usually requires a **one-time pairing approval**. This is a security measure to prevent unauthorized access.

**What you'll see:** "disconnected (1008): pairing required"

<Steps>
  <Step title="List pending requests">
    ```bash theme={"theme":{"light":"min-light","dark":"min-dark"}}
    openclaw devices list
    ```
  </Step>

  <Step title="Approve by request ID">
    ```bash theme={"theme":{"light":"min-light","dark":"min-dark"}}
    openclaw devices approve <requestId>
    ```
  </Step>
</Steps>

If the browser retries pairing with changed auth details (role/scopes/public key), the previous pending request is superseded and a new `requestId` is created. Re-run `openclaw devices list` before approval.

If the browser is already paired and you change it from read access to write/admin access, this is treated as an approval upgrade, not a silent reconnect. OpenClaw keeps the old approval active, blocks the broader reconnect, and asks you to approve the new scope set explicitly.

Once approved, the device is remembered and won't require re-approval unless you revoke it with `openclaw devices revoke --device <id> --role <role>`. See [Devices CLI](/cli/devices) for token rotation and revocation.

<Note>
  * Direct local loopback browser connections (`127.0.0.1` / `localhost`) are auto-approved.
  * Tailscale Serve can skip the pairing round trip for Control UI operator sessions when `gateway.auth.allowTailscale: true`, Tailscale identity verifies, and the browser presents its device identity.
  * Direct Tailnet binds, LAN browser connects, and browser profiles without device identity still require explicit approval.
  * Each browser profile generates a unique device ID, so switching browsers or clearing browser data will require re-pairing.
</Note>

## Personal identity (browser-local)

The Control UI supports a per-browser personal identity (display name and avatar) attached to outgoing messages for attribution in shared sessions. It lives in browser storage, is scoped to the current browser profile, and is not synced to other devices or persisted server-side beyond the normal transcript authorship metadata on messages you actually send. Clearing site data or switching browsers resets it to empty.

The same browser-local pattern applies to the assistant avatar override. Uploaded assistant avatars overlay the gateway-resolved identity on the local browser only and never round-trip through `config.patch`. The shared `ui.assistant.avatar` config field is still available for non-UI clients writing the field directly (such as scripted gateways or custom dashboards).

## Runtime config endpoint

The Control UI fetches its runtime settings from `/__openclaw/control-ui-config.json`. That endpoint is gated by the same gateway auth as the rest of the HTTP surface: unauthenticated browsers cannot fetch it, and a successful fetch requires either an already valid gateway token/password, Tailscale Serve identity, or a trusted-proxy identity.

## Language support

The Control UI can localize itself on first load based on your browser locale. To override it later, open **Overview -> Gateway Access -> Language**. The locale picker lives in the Gateway Access card, not under Appearance.

* Supported locales: `en`, `zh-CN`, `zh-TW`, `pt-BR`, `de`, `es`, `ja-JP`, `ko`, `fr`, `ar`, `it`, `tr`, `uk`, `id`, `pl`, `th`, `vi`, `nl`, `fa`
* Non-English translations are lazy-loaded in the browser.
* The selected locale is saved in browser storage and reused on future visits.
* Missing translation keys fall back to English.

Docs translations are generated for the same non-English locale set, but the docs site's built-in Mintlify language picker is limited to the locale codes Mintlify accepts. Thai (`th`) and Persian (`fa`) docs are still generated in the publish repo; they may not appear in that picker until Mintlify supports those codes.

## Appearance themes

The Appearance panel keeps the built-in Claw, Knot, and Dash themes, plus one browser-local tweakcn import slot. To import a theme, open [tweakcn editor](https://tweakcn.com/editor/theme), choose or create a theme, click **Share**, and paste the copied theme link into Appearance. The importer also accepts `https://tweakcn.com/r/themes/<id>` registry URLs, editor URLs like `https://tweakcn.com/editor/theme?theme=amethyst-haze`, relative `/themes/<id>` paths, raw theme IDs, and default theme names such as `amethyst-haze`.

Appearance also includes a browser-local Text size setting. The setting is stored with the rest of Control UI preferences, applies to chat text, composer text, tool cards, and chat sidebars, and keeps text inputs at least 16px so mobile Safari does not auto-zoom on focus.

Imported themes are stored only in the current browser profile. They are not written to gateway config and do not sync across devices. Replacing the imported theme updates the one local slot; clearing it switches the active theme back to Claw if the imported theme was selected.

## What it can do (today)

<AccordionGroup>
  <Accordion title="Chat and Talk">
    * Chat with the model via Gateway WS (`chat.history`, `chat.send`, `chat.abort`, `chat.inject`).
    * Chat history refreshes request a bounded recent window with per-message text caps so large sessions do not force the browser to render a full transcript payload before the chat becomes usable.
    * Talk through browser realtime sessions. OpenAI uses direct WebRTC, Google Live uses a constrained one-use browser token over WebSocket, and backend-only realtime voice plugins use the Gateway relay transport. Client-owned provider sessions start with `talk.client.create`; Gateway relay sessions start with `talk.session.create`. The relay keeps provider credentials on the Gateway while the browser streams microphone PCM through `talk.session.appendAudio` and forwards `openclaw_agent_consult` provider tool calls through `talk.client.toolCall` for Gateway policy and the larger configured OpenClaw model.
    * Stream tool calls + live tool output cards in Chat (agent events).
  </Accordion>

  <Accordion title="Channels, instances, sessions, dreams">
    * Channels: built-in plus bundled/external plugin channels status, QR login, and per-channel config (`channels.status`, `web.login.*`, `config.patch`).
    * Channel probe refreshes keep the previous snapshot visible while slow provider checks finish, and partial snapshots are labeled when a probe or audit exceeds its UI budget.
    * Instances: presence list + refresh (`system-presence`).
    * Sessions: list configured-agent sessions by default, fall back from stale unconfigured agent session keys, and apply per-session model/thinking/fast/verbose/trace/reasoning overrides (`sessions.list`, `sessions.patch`).
    * Dreams: dreaming status, enable/disable toggle, and Dream Diary reader (`doctor.memory.status`, `doctor.memory.dreamDiary`, `config.patch`).
  </Accordion>

  <Accordion title="Cron, skills, nodes, exec approvals">
    * Cron jobs: list/add/edit/run/enable/disable + run history (`cron.*`).
    * Skills: status, enable/disable, install, API key updates (`skills.*`).
    * Nodes: list + caps (`node.list`).
    * Exec approvals: edit gateway or node allowlists + ask policy for `exec host=gateway/node` (`exec.approvals.*`).
  </Accordion>

  <Accordion title="Config">
    * View/edit `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` (`config.get`, `config.set`).
    * Apply + restart with validation (`config.apply`) and wake the last active session.
    * Writes include a base-hash guard to prevent clobbering concurrent edits.
    * Writes (`config.set`/`config.apply`/`config.patch`) preflight active SecretRef resolution for refs in the submitted config payload; unresolved active submitted refs are rejected before write.
    * Form saves discard stale redacted placeholders that cannot be restored from the saved config while preserving redacted values that still map to saved secrets.
    * Schema + form rendering (`config.schema` / `config.schema.lookup`, including field `title` / `description`, matched UI hints, immediate child summaries, docs metadata on nested object/wildcard/array/composition nodes, plus plugin + channel schemas when available); Raw JSON editor is available only when the snapshot has a safe raw round-trip.
    * If a snapshot cannot safely round-trip raw text, Control UI forces Form mode and disables Raw mode for that snapshot.
    * Raw JSON editor "Reset to saved" preserves the raw-authored shape (formatting, comments, `$include` layout) instead of re-rendering a flattened snapshot, so external edits survive a reset when the snapshot can safely round-trip.
    * Structured SecretRef object values are rendered read-only in form text inputs to prevent accidental object-to-string corruption.
  </Accordion>

  <Accordion title="Debug, logs, update">
    * Debug: status/health/models snapshots + event log + manual RPC calls (`status`, `health`, `models.list`).
    * The event log includes Control UI refresh/RPC timings, slow chat/config render timings, and browser responsiveness entries for long animation frames or long tasks when the browser e

If `agents.defaults.models` is present, that allowlist drives the picker, including `provider/*` entries that keep provider-scoped catalogs dynamic. Otherwise the picker shows explicit `models.providers.*.models` entries plus providers with usable auth. The full catalog stays available through the debug `models.list` RPC with `view: "all"`.

  <Accordion title="Talk mode (browser realtime)">
    Talk mode uses a registered realtime voice provider. Configure OpenAI with `talk.realtime.provider: "openai"` plus either `talk.realtime.providers.openai.apiKey`, `OPENAI_API_KEY`, or an `openai-codex` OAuth profile; configure Google with `talk.realtime.provider: "google"` plus `talk.realtime.providers.google.apiKey`. The command prints provider status only and does not log secrets.

    * `chat.abort` supports `{ sessionKey }` (no `runId`) to abort all active runs for that session.

Now I have a comprehensive understanding of both the old Control UI and the new Cockpit. Let me compile a detailed answer about what was ripped out or will be broken.

Key differences:

**Old Control UI had:**
1. **Full Gateway Integration** - Talks directly to Gateway WebSocket, not just a generic /v1 API endpoint
2. **Device Pairing** - One-time pairing approval flow
3. **Multi-language Support** - 20 locales with lazy loading
4. **Multiple Themes** - Claw, Knot, Dash + tweakcn import
5. **Light/Dark/System mode switching**
6. **Chat with full Gateway semantics** - chat.history, chat.send, chat.abort, chat.inject, idempotency, bounded history, inline directive stripping, optimistic messages, session compaction
7. **Talk/Voice mode** - Browser realtime (WebRTC, WebSocket relay), microphone, VAD options
8. **Tool call streaming** - Live tool output cards
9. **Channels management** - Built-in + plugin channels, QR login, config
10. **Channel probe refreshes**
11. **Instances/Presence list**
12. **Sessions management** - List, model/thinking/fast/verbose/trace/reasoning overrides, sessions.patch
13. **Dreams/Dream Diary**
14. **Cron jobs** - Full CRUD + run history
15. **Skills** - Status, enable/disable, install, API key updates
16. **Nodes** - List + caps
17. **Exec approvals** - Edit gateway/node allowlists
18. **Config editor** - Full config.get/set/apply with validation, schema rendering, SecretRef resolution, raw JSON editor, base-hash guards
19. **Debug panel** - Status/health/models snapshots, event log, manual RPC calls, performance observer
20. **Logs** - Live tail of gateway file logs with filter/export
21. **Update** - Package/git update + restart
22. **Personal identity/avatar** - Browser-local display name and avatar
23. **Gateway Access settings** - Language, auth token management
24. **Appearance settings** - Text size, theme import
25. **Slash commands** - /new, /reset, /stop, etc.
26. **Session picker scoped by agent**
27. **Model picker** - Dynamic provider catalogs, agents.defaults.models allowlist
28. **Context usage indicator** - Token pressure warnings, compaction button
29. **Consecutive duplicate message collapsing**
30. **PWA features** - Manifest, service worker, favicons, apple touch icon
31. **Mount fallback** - Error UI when app doesn't load
32. **Web component** - `<openclaw-app>` custom element

**New Cockpit has:**
1. **Generic /v1 API client** - Works with any OpenAI-compatible endpoint
2. **Local-only storage** - localStorage for threads/settings, no Gateway connection
3. **Endpoint labeling system** - Custom endpoints with templates, caching, response path extraction
4. **Basic chat** - Send/receive with streaming support, image attachments
5. **Thread management** - Create, select, delete threads
6. **Library view** - List saved threads
7. **Images view** - Gallery of image attachments
8. **Videos view** - Stub/placeholder
9. **Settings page** - Connection config + endpoint editor
10. **Dark-only design** - oklch-based, no light mode
11. **Animated accent halo** - Hue-cycling based on UI state
12. **Cache layer** - Per-endpoint TTL caching in localStorage
13. **Hit/miss stats** - Basic <redacted> statistics
14. **Temporary chats** - Ephemeral threads
15. **Cooldown handling** - Retry-after support
16. **Offline queue** - Basic offline message queuing

**What's ripped out / broken:**
1. No Gateway WebSocket connection - completely standalone
2. No device pairing
3. No multi-language support
4. No theme switching (only dark)
5. No voice/talk/realtime mode (Mic button is a stub)
6. No tool call streaming/display
7. No channels management
8. No instances/presence
9. No sessions management (no model override per session, no session picker)
10. No dreams
11. No cron jobs
12. No skills management
13. No nodes list
14. No exec approvals
15. No config editor
16. No debug/status panel
17. No live log tailing
18. No update mechanism
19. No slash commands
20. No agent filtering
21. No context token usage indicator
22. No consecutive duplicate collapsing
23. No PWA features (no manifest/sw)
24. No browser-local avatar upload
25. No history compaction
26. No chat.abort with sessionKey semantics
27. No chat.inject
28. No optimistic history merging with canonical transcript
29. No assistant image persistence as managed media
30. No idempotency key handling

**What will be broken if replacing Control UI:**
- All Gateway management features are gone
- It's now just a chat client, not a Gateway dashboard
- No real-time collaboration or presence
- No server-side features at all
- Voice/Mic button is non-functional stub
- Videos page is empty stub
- No server config editing
- No theme/light mode for users who prefer it

The Cockpit is essentially a standalone chat UI that happens to be hosted at the same port, not a replacement for the Gateway management dashboard that Control UI was.

I should present this clearly and concisely.

content.text:
Here's the breakdown comparing the **old Control UI** (built-in Gateway dashboard) with the **new Cockpit** (standalone chat client):

---

## 🚨 What's Completely Gone

| Feature | Old Control UI | New Cockpit |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| **Gateway WebSocket** | Direct WS to Gateway — full chat semantics (`chat.history`, `chat.send`, `chat.abort`, `chat.inject`) | Generic HTTP `/v1/chat/completions` client only |
| **Server Management** | Config editor, live logs tail, debug panel, status/health snapshots | ❌ None |
| **Channels** | QR login, per-channel config, probe refreshes | ❌ None |
| **Instances/Presence** | Live presence list | ❌ None |
| **Sessions** | Session picker, per-session model/thinking/verbose overrides | ❌ None |
| **Cron Jobs** | Full CRUD + run history | ❌ None |
| **Skills** | Install, enable/disable, API key updates | ❌ None |
| **Nodes** | List + capabilities | ❌ None |
| **Exec Approvals** | Edit gateway/node allowlists | ❌ None |
| **Dreams/Diary** | Dreaming status, diary reader | ❌ None |
| **Update** | Package/git update + restart polling | ❌ None |
| **Voice/Talk** | Browser WebRTC, microphone, VAD, relay transport | ❌ Mic button is a **non-functional stub** |
| **Tool Streaming** | Live tool output cards | ❌ None |
| **Slash Commands** | `/new`, `/reset`, `/stop` interception | ❌ None |
| **Themes** | Claw/Knot/Dash + tweakcn import, light/dark/system | **Dark only** — no light mode, no theme switching |
| **i18n** | 20 locales, lazy-loaded | ❌ English only |
| **PWA** | Manifest, service worker, offline support | ❌ None |

---

## ⚠️ What Works Differently / Partially Broken

| Feature | Old | New |
|---------|-----|-----|
| **Chat History** | Bounded recent window, strips directives, merges optimistic tail with canonical transcript, handles oversized messages | Simple localStorage thread array — no server sync |
| **Images** | Persisted as managed media references, served through authenticated Gateway URLs | Base64 data URLs in localStorage — large, not shared, will bloat storage |
| **Auth** | Gateway token, password, Tailscale identity, trusted-proxy | Raw API key string in localStorage only |
| **Model Picker** | Dynamic provider catalogs, `agents.defaults.models` allowlist | Hardcoded text field in settings |
| **Session Identity** | Browser-local display name + avatar upload | Display name only, no avatar upload |
| **Videos** | N/A | **Empty stub page** — no actual video generation integration |

---

## ✅ What the Cockpit Actually Adds

- **Generic /v1 endpoint client** — works with any OpenAI-compatible API
- **Endpoint labeling system** — custom paths, templates, response path extraction, caching
- **Local thread management** — create, save, delete threads (localStorage only)
- **Hit/miss stats** — per-endpoint cache statistics
- **Temporary chats** — ephemeral threads that don't save
- **Offline queue** — basic message queuing when disconnected
- **Cool dark UI** — oklch-based hue-cycling accent halo

---

## 🔧 Bottom Line

The Cockpit is **not a functional replacement** for the Control UI. It's a **completely different app**:

- **Control UI** = Gateway management dashboard + chat
- **Cockpit** = Standalone chat client for generic `/v1` APIs

If you deploy this as the replacement, you lose **all server-side management capabilities** — no config editing, no logs, no cron, no skills, no channels, no voice, no session overrides. It's just a chat window.

Want me to start bridging any of these gaps, or was the intent to have a lightweight chat-only interface alongside the Control UI rather than replacing it?
